title: The Strategic Retrospective of Cognitive Systems (2022–2026)
subtitle: A comprehensive analysis of global compute infrastructure, architectural transitions, operational economics, and geopolitical splits.

macroShifts.title: Generative Mimicry to Agentic Autonomy
macroShifts.desc: The paradigm shifted from stateless text prediction ('probabilistic parrots') to stateful 'System 2' deliberation engines. Modern agents orchestrate multi-turn planning, hidden chain-of-thought verification, and desktop/OS navigation, officially outperforming human baselines on physical and digital environments.
points: System 2 reasoning introduced by o-series, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, and Kimi K2.
GPT-5.4 (March 2026) achieves 75.0% on OSWorld-Verified, outperforming the 72.4% human average.
Yann LeCun raises $1.03B for AMI Labs, engineering physical world models from sensory inputs rather than next-token predictions.

macroShifts.title: The Strategic Inversion of Weights
macroShifts.desc: Sovereign open-weight infrastructures successfully closed the intelligence gap with centralized APIs (collapsing the MMLU gap to under 0.3%). Organizations are shifting workloads from metered APIs to on-premises and air-gapped local clusters, reporting a massive 90% reduction in computing costs.
points: DeepSeek V3.2, Qwen3-235B, and LLaMA 4 Scout achieve near-parity with GPT-5.2 and Claude 4.
Heavy workloads costing $2,275/month on closed APIs drop to just $168/month on open infrastructures (13-fold savings).
Provides absolute data sovereignty and local fine-tuning freedom indefinitely, avoiding deprecation risks.

macroShifts.title: The Silicon Interconnect Arms War
macroShifts.desc: Global microchip manufacturing transitioned from raw GPU accelerator scaling to system-level interconnect configurations. The bottleneck is no longer just memory volumes but memory-flop utilization (MFU) driven by NVLink fabric bandwidth and Google's Optical Circuit Switching (OCS) — and increasingly, by the gigawatts of data-center capacity available to power them.
points: Google TPU v7 Ironwood (GA Nov 2025) connects 9,216 liquid-cooled chips per pod via OCS, delivering 42.5 ExaFLOPS FP8 with 1.77 PB pod HBM3e — Anthropic signed a $52B deal for up to 1M Ironwood chips.
Nvidia's NVLink 5 enables 1.8 TB/s per-GPU bandwidth on Blackwell B200; OpenAI's Stargate Abilene campus alone will host 450,000 GB200 GPUs at 1.2 GW.
AMD Instinct MI350X provides 288 GB HBM3e and 8 TB/s memory bandwidth per chip; AMD locked in 6 GW of OpenAI capacity (Oct 2025).
Custom silicon goes mainstream: Microsoft Maia 200 (216 GB HBM3e, 10+ PFLOPS FP4, Jan 2026), Amazon Trainium 3, OpenAI's 'Titan' chip with Broadcom (TSMC 3nm, H2 2026).

macroShifts.title: Economic Deflation & Workforce Bifurcation
macroShifts.desc: Deep integration of connectionist systems has unlocked record corporate revenue efficiencies while triggering structural deflation. While junior developer entry pipelines contracted severely, specialized positions in system design architecture, local RAG engineering, and cybersecurity are seeing historic wage premiums.
points: AI code generators generate 46% of active files (61% for Java), cutting coding time by 55%.
Junior developer hiring demand has contracted by 40% in AI-forward firms.
Average specialist salaries hit $206,000 (56% premium) for custom tuning and RAG orchestration.

macroShifts.title: Healthcare X-Factor & Scientific Leap
macroShifts.desc: Clinical assistants and ambient scribes transformed healthcare business models from human-intensive administrative layers to automated processing. Concurrently, biochemical models accelerated the drug discovery pipeline by automating biologic expression predictions.
points: Ambient scribing tools cut medical documentation time by 50% to 70% in clinical exam rooms.
Health-tech business revenue efficiency spiked from $100K up to $1M+ ARR per employee.
MIT generative models automated biologic codon optimization, replacing multi-year trials with single-pass rankings.

macroShifts.title: Model → Harness → Ecosystem
macroShifts.desc: The product is no longer the model alone — it is the agentic harness that wraps it. By May 2026, AI infrastructure had stratified into a six-layer stack (permissions, memory, execution, integration, multi-agent, observability), with two open inter-op protocols (MCP, A2A) under Linux Foundation governance and a $12.5B-by-2030 multi-agent orchestration market.
points: Anthropic ships 'Dreaming' (May 6, 2026) — async hippocampal consolidation between agent sessions; Harvey reports 6× task lift.
MCP servers cross 25,000 in the Glama Registry; A2A (agent-to-agent) joins MCP under Linux Foundation governance.
OpenClaw — open-source personal AI gateway — explodes from launch (Nov 2025) to 375K GitHub stars in ~6 months, sponsored by OpenAI, GitHub, NVIDIA, Vercel.
Cognition (Devin) talks at $25B valuation after 73× ARR growth; SpaceX agrees to acquire Cursor for $60B (April 2026).

macroShifts.title: Regulatory & Geopolitical Bifurcation
macroShifts.desc: The international legal and compliance landscape fractured into two opposing blocks: the prescriptive, risk-based European framework enforcing safety, and the innovation-first United States model preempting local state restrictions to accelerate raw computing dominance.
points: EU AI Act (August 2026 fully applicable) completely bans 8 practices (manipulation, biometric tracking, social scoring).
US Executive Order 14365 (December 2025) challenge state-level regulations, using BEAD infrastructure funding as leverage.
Zero-Trust cybersecurity architectures replace basic authentication to counter Deepfake-as-a-Service (DaaS) fraud.

fullEvolutionTimeline.landmark: Turing Test paper (1950), ELIZA conversational mimicry (1966)
fullEvolutionTimeline.limitation: Extreme computational bottlenecks, brittle static rules, triggered the first AI Winter (1974)

fullEvolutionTimeline.landmark: Prolog language (1972), DEC XCON commercial Expert System
fullEvolutionTimeline.limitation: High maintenance costs, lack of dynamic learning capability, triggered second deeper winter

fullEvolutionTimeline.mechanism: Backpropagation error tuning, sequential matrix computations
fullEvolutionTimeline.landmark: IBM Deep Blue Garry Kasparov defeat (1997), ImageNet database (2009), Apple Siri (2011)
fullEvolutionTimeline.limitation: High requirement for manual feature engineering, severe dataset scarcity limits deep layer sizing

fullEvolutionTimeline.mechanism: Parallelized matrix multiplications accelerated via hardware GPUs
fullEvolutionTimeline.landmark: AlexNet ImageNet victory (2012), AlphaGo Lee Sedol defeat (2016), Google Transformer (2017)
fullEvolutionTimeline.limitation: Context window constraints, sequential processing bottlenecks in recurrent networks

fullEvolutionTimeline.landmark: GPT-3 (2020), ChatGPT consumer launch (Nov 2022), GPT-4 native multimodality (2023)
fullEvolutionTimeline.limitation: Hallucinations, stateless prompt context limitations, failure on multi-step logical tasks

fullEvolutionTimeline.mechanism: Internal logical reasoning, hidden System 2 chain-of-thought loops, dynamic tool-calling
fullEvolutionTimeline.landmark: Claude 3.7 Sonnet (2025), DeepSeek V3.2 MoE (2025), GPT-5.4 Deliberation (March 2026)
fullEvolutionTimeline.limitation: High inference compute latency, complex session state management across hundreds of tool loops

2022.title: ChatGPT Democratization Boom
2022.desc: OpenAI releases ChatGPT (GPT-3.5) in November, demonstrating human-like chat and reaching 100M active users in 2 months, shifting NLP into standard conversational workflows.

2022.title: AlphaFold 2 Protein Synthesis
2022.desc: Google DeepMind releases structural predictions for 200M+ proteins (nearly all known cataloged proteins), revolutionizing structural biology.

2022.title: High-Fidelity Diffusion Models
2022.desc: Stable Diffusion, Midjourney, and DALL-E 2 launch, democratizing photorealistic image synthesis and showing early scaling capabilities.

2023.title: Frontier Multimodality (GPT-4)
2023.desc: GPT-4 launches in March, establishing a historic benchmark in logical reasoning, document structural analysis, and multi-step coding logic.

2023.title: The Llama Catalyst
2023.desc: Meta open-sources LLaMA, triggering an open-weight developer explosion. Mobile platforms (Snapdragon) begin hosting smaller models locally.

2023.title: Early Stateful Prototyping
2023.desc: Projects like AutoGPT and BabyAGI spark global interest in autonomous agentic loops, though hindered by stateless prompts and high hallucination rates.

2023.title: US & UK AI Safety Initiatives
2023.desc: NIST launches the U.S. AI Safety Institute, and the UK hosts the Bletchley Park Summit to address high-risk frontier model capabilities.

2024.title: Sora Video Realism
2024.desc: OpenAI showcases Sora in February, generating photorealistic, temporally consistent 60-second video clips via video-diffusion-transformer architectures.

2024.title: Claude 3 & Advanced Reasoning
2024.desc: Anthropic releases the Claude 3 family, with Opus establishing a new state-of-the-art benchmark for graduate-level reasoning and safety.

2024.title: EU AI Act Enacted
2024.desc: The European Union officially adopts the risk-based AI Act in May (entering into force in August 2024), establishing global legal precedents.

2024.title: Desktop Computer Use
2024.desc: Anthropic launches Claude 'Computer Use' in October, giving the model direct control over mouse movements, typing, and desktop navigation.

2025.title: Reasoning Cost Deflation
2025.desc: DeepSeek V3 and Qwen 2.5 launch, demonstrating massive architectural efficiencies and achieving high performance at a fraction of US training costs.

2025.title: System 2 Deliberation & o-series
2025.desc: Centralized providers launch models with hidden reinforcement learning-driven chain-of-thought execution, allowing deep planning before token output.

2025.title: MMLU Gap Collapses
2025.desc: Open-weight models reduce the performance gap against proprietary APIs to less than 0.3%, sparking a rapid corporate migration to self-hosted clusters.

2025.title: Federal Preservation Order
2025.desc: In May, a federal judge orders OpenAI to preserve and segregate all conversation logs as evidence in the landmark New York Times lawsuit.

2025.title: MCP & A2A Become Open Standards
2025.desc: Anthropic's Model Context Protocol (Nov 2024) and Google's Agent-to-Agent protocol (Apr 2025) move under Linux Foundation governance, formalizing the tool↔model and agent↔agent integration layers across Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Microsoft, and AWS.

2026.title: OSWorld Human Baseline Beaten
2026.desc: GPT-5.4 launches in March, scoring 75.0% on the OSWorld-Verified benchmark, officially outperforming the 72.4% average human baseline at direct computer navigation.

2026.title: Anthropic Ships 'Dreaming'
2026.desc: On May 6, Anthropic introduces async hippocampal memory consolidation for Claude Managed Agents — a between-session process that reorganizes memory like sleep. Harvey reports a 6× task-completion lift on production legal agents.

2026.title: Cognition (Devin) at $25B Talks
2026.desc: After 73× ARR growth in 9 months ($1M → $73M+), Cognition enters talks at a $25B valuation (Apr 2026). Two days earlier, SpaceX agreed to acquire Cursor (Anysphere) for $60B — anchoring agentic-coding as the killer app.

2026.title: Tesla Fremont optimus Deployments
2026.desc: Tesla deploys over 1,000 Optimus Gen 3 humanoid units at its Fremont automotive manufacturing facility, performing structured physical assembly tasks.

2026.title: Clinical Trial Compression
2026.desc: Isomorphic Labs (Google DeepMind) enters first-in-human clinical trials for drugs designed completely via automated AlphaFold molecular workflows.

2026.title: OpenClaw Star Surge
2026.desc: Personal AI gateway OpenClaw (created November 2025) crosses 375,000 GitHub stars by May 2026 — roughly six months — making it one of the fastest-growing open-source projects in history. It is sponsored by OpenAI, GitHub, NVIDIA, and Vercel, attracts 2,238 contributors, and spawns a sub-ecosystem (NVIDIA NemoClaw, Cloudflare moltworker, ClawHub registry). It demonstrates demand for self-hosted, multi-channel personal AI harnesses outside the major-vendor stacks.

2026.title: US Executive Order 14365
2026.desc: Issued in December 2025, the order establishes a unified national 'innovation-first' policy, challenging state-level AI regulations under threat of telecom funding withholding.

system2Explainer.title: System 2 Reasoning
system2Explainer.subtitle: From cognitive psychology to AI's deliberation revolution
system2Explainer.tldr: System 1 is the gut. System 2 is the deliberation. The shorthand survived the jump from Kahneman's psychology to modern reasoning models because it captures what users actually feel: the model thinks before it speaks.

origin.heading: Origin: Kahneman's dual-process theory
origin.body: The terminology comes from cognitive psychology. Nobel laureate Daniel Kahneman popularised it in his 2011 book Thinking, Fast and Slow. The labels 'System 1' and 'System 2' themselves were introduced earlier by psychologists Keith Stanovich and Richard West around 2000, building on decades of work by Kahneman and Amos Tversky on cognitive biases. Kahneman didn't invent the dual-process model — but his book is what made it a household concept.

comparison.heading: The two systems at a glance

rows.s2: '17 × 24' in your head · parking in a tight space
Mental fatigue, lazy fall-back to System 1

aiCrossover.heading: How the metaphor crossed over into AI
aiCrossover.body: By the late 2010s, deep-learning researchers — most prominently Turing-Award winner Yoshua Bengio — borrowed the framing to describe a limitation of large language models. Bengio's influential 2019 NeurIPS keynote, 'From System 1 Deep Learning to System 2 Deep Learning,' made the framing canonical in the field.
aiCrossover.argument: LLMs of that era (GPT-2, GPT-3) were essentially gigantic System 1 machines: fluent, plausible answers from pattern-matching on training data, no internal mechanism to step back and verify, and predictable failure on multi-step logic, planning, or out-of-distribution reasoning. This earned them the dismissive label 'stochastic parrots' (Bender et al., 2021) — a System-1-only mind that mimics fluency but doesn't deliberate.

modernMeaning.title: Hidden chain-of-thought
modernMeaning.desc: The model produces a long internal scratchpad of reasoning steps before emitting a final answer. The user sees the answer; the model spent thousands of tokens deliberating.

modernMeaning.title: Test-time compute scaling
modernMeaning.desc: Instead of investing more compute at training time, you invest at inference time. A System 2 model spends more 'thinking' on a hard problem.

modernMeaning.title: Self-verification & backtracking
modernMeaning.desc: The model can detect when its own intermediate step is wrong and explore alternatives, rather than committing to the first plausible token sequence.

modernMeaning.title: Deliberation budget
modernMeaning.desc: Most reasoning models expose a 'thinking effort' knob so users can trade latency and cost for answer quality on a per-request basis.

landmarks.name: OpenAI o1
landmarks.note: First major commercial model marketed explicitly on its System 2 reasoning, especially on math, science, and coding benchmarks.

landmarks.name: OpenAI o3
landmarks.note: Pushed the envelope further; o3 was the first model to crack the ARC-AGI benchmark.

landmarks.name: DeepSeek-R1
landmarks.note: Watershed moment for open-source: System 2 reasoning trained via pure reinforcement learning at a fraction of the budget, with open weights.

landmarks.name: Claude 3.7 Sonnet
landmarks.note: Anthropic's first hybrid reasoning model with an 'extended thinking' mode users could toggle.

landmarks.name: Kimi K2 → K2.6
landmarks.note: Chinese-lab System 2 with native tool-calling. K2.6 ties GPT-5.5 on SWE-Bench Pro at 80% lower cost.

landmarks.name: Qwen 3.7 Max
landmarks.note: Native extended-thinking mode by default; 35-hour autonomous coding demo with 1,000+ tool calls.

caveat.heading: What's actually happening under the hood
caveat.body: It's a useful shorthand, but it's not literally true that LLMs have two systems. A normal LLM forward pass produces one token at a time using the same neural network, no matter the difficulty. A 'System 2' model uses the same network but is trained — often via reinforcement learning — to first generate a long reasoning trace, and only then commit to a final answer. The 'two systems' are really one system used in two different inference patterns: one shallow, one deep. The Kahneman label survives because it captures the user-visible behaviour — the model genuinely 'thinks before it speaks' — even if the brain analogy is loose.

frontierModels2026.name: GPT-5.5
frontierModels2026.capabilities: Strongest one-shot reasoning, native computer use, multimodal. Long-Horizon Coding bench: 70% (vs Claude Opus 4.7 at 54%, Gemini 3.1 Pro at 10%). Pricing: $5 input / $30 output per Mtok.

frontierModels2026.name: DeepSeek V4 Pro Max
frontierModels2026.capabilities: Open-weight (MIT-style); 75% permanent price cut to $1.74 / $3.48 per Mtok (May 2026). Trained on 750K chips / 140T tokens. The cost-efficiency benchmark for agentic workloads.

frontierModels2026.name: Qwen 3.7 Max
frontierModels2026.capabilities: Agent-first design with native extended-thinking mode. Demo: ran autonomously for 35 hours writing kernels for Alibaba's Zhenwu M890 chip (1,000+ tool calls). Lowest hallucination rate at 22.9%. Closed-weight, $2.50/$7.50 per Mtok on DashScope.

frontierModels2026.name: Kimi K2.6
frontierModels2026.gpqa: 90.5% GPQA · 58.6% SWE-Bench Pro (ties GPT-5.5) · 54.0% HLE w/ tools
frontierModels2026.capabilities: Open-weight (Modified MIT). Agent Swarm: orchestrates up to 300 parallel sub-agents (4.5× faster vs sequential). Pricing: $0.95/$4.00 per Mtok — 80% cheaper than GPT-5.5 on tied benchmarks.

frontierModels2026.name: GLM-5.1
frontierModels2026.capabilities: Open-weight Chinese frontier model with strong tool-use and SWE-Bench performance (predecessor GLM-5: 77.8%). Pricing: $1.40/$4.40 per Mtok. Strong interleaved multimodal reasoning.

frontierModels2026.name: Llama 4 Scout
frontierModels2026.gpqa: Solid mid-tier; weakest of the six on coding leaderboards
frontierModels2026.capabilities: Holds the announced context-window record at 10M tokens — built for whole-codebase / whole-document analysis. INT4-quantized form runs on a single H100. Open-weight, the long-context baseline for the open-source ecosystem.

benchmarkSota.peakClosedModel: GPT-5.2 Pro / GPT-5.2 / Gemini 3 Pro / Grok-4 Heavy

agenticStack.title: The Agentic Stack
agenticStack.intro: The model is no longer the product — the harness around it is. Between 2024 and 2026, AI infrastructure stratified into a six-layer agentic stack, two open inter-op protocols (MCP, A2A) under Linux Foundation governance, six competing memory primitives, four orchestration frameworks, and a $12.5B-by-2030 multi-agent coordination market. Click any number to verify against its source.

harnessLayers.name: Input
harnessLayers.primitives: Permission modes (default/plan/auto/dontAsk), fine-grained rules, project trust, .claude/settings.json

harnessLayers.name: Knowledge
harnessLayers.primitives: CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, Skills (SKILL.md), auto-memory, compaction, prompt caching

harnessLayers.name: Execution
harnessLayers.purpose: Tool dispatch and the agentic loop
harnessLayers.primitives: Built-in tools (Read/Edit/Write/Bash/Grep/Glob), parallel reads, sequential mutations, ~90% cache reads

harnessLayers.name: Integration
harnessLayers.primitives: MCP servers, plugins, channels, MCP Tool Search (deferred schema loading)

harnessLayers.name: Multi-Agent
harnessLayers.purpose: Delegated work in isolated context windows
harnessLayers.primitives: Subagents (Agent tool), agent teams, git worktree isolation, parallel runners

harnessLayers.name: Observability
harnessLayers.primitives: Hooks (PreToolUse/PostToolUse/Stop/PreCompact), checkpoints, session JSONL, max_turns / max_budget_usd

loop.title: The Continuous Agent Loop
loop.desc: Every modern agentic harness — Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Devin, Goose, Aider — runs the same core cycle: gather context, take action, verify results. The harness mediates each step; the model only chooses the next move.

phases.tools: Read, Grep, Glob, web fetch, MCP search
Edit, Write, Bash, MCP actions, sub-agent dispatch
Re-run tests, lint, hooks, max_turns / budget guard

protocols.name: MCP — Model Context Protocol
protocols.purpose: Standardizes how models discover and call external tools, data sources, and execution environments via JSON-RPC.
stats: Adopted by Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, Microsoft, AWS
MCP Tool Search defers schema load to keep idle servers out of context

protocols.name: A2A — Agent-to-Agent Protocol
protocols.purpose: Standardizes inter-agent task delegation, negotiation, and result reporting over HTTP/SSE — the agent↔agent counterpart to MCP's tool↔model layer.
stats: Complementary to MCP (tools vs agent coordination)
Counterpart efforts: ACP (Paperclip / Nous Research), ANP (Microsoft)

memoryArchitectures.name: Letta (MemGPT lineage)
memoryArchitectures.highlight: Three-tier core/archival/recall; agent-managed promotion between tiers

memoryArchitectures.name: Zep (Graphiti)
memoryArchitectures.highlight: Timestamped node/edge updates; strongest on temporal recall ("yesterday vs today")

memoryArchitectures.name: Mem0
memoryArchitectures.highlight: 41K stars, 14M downloads; integrates Anthropic SDK / OpenAI Agents / Google ADK

memoryArchitectures.name: Anthropic Dreaming
memoryArchitectures.highlight: Between-session replay reorganizes memory like sleep — explicit hippocampal model (May 6, 2026)

memoryArchitectures.name: Google Memory Bank
memoryArchitectures.highlight: Native ADK 2.0 primitive; per-user facts/preferences across sessions

memoryArchitectures.name: OpenAI file_search
memoryArchitectures.highlight: previous_response_id for sessions; vector store is the long-term memory primitive

frameworkAdoption.sourceLabel: IDC FutureScape: AI Agents 2025 / ECOA AI 2026

marketTrajectory.headline: $1M → $73M ARR in 9 months
marketTrajectory.detail: Founded by 3 IOI gold medalists. Goldman Sachs piloting Devin alongside 12,000 human developers — 20% efficiency target.

marketTrajectory.headline: SpaceX agreed to acquire
marketTrajectory.detail: AI-native IDE. One of the largest private acquisitions in AI history; reset pricing anchors across the agentic-coding category.

marketTrajectory.headline: SWE-1.5 at 950 tokens/sec
marketTrajectory.detail: Devin built into the IDE; Agent Command Center coordinates parallel agent sessions. Released April 2026.

marketTrajectory.headline: 0 → 375K stars in ~6 months
marketTrajectory.detail: Personal AI gateway by Peter Steinberger — Claude-Code-style six-layer harness wired into 23 messaging channels (WhatsApp / Telegram / Slack / Discord / Signal / iMessage / WeChat …) plus Voice Wake and Live Canvas. ClawHub skill registry, awesome-openclaw-skills (5,400+ skills, 49K stars), NVIDIA NemoClaw, Cloudflare moltworker. One of the fastest GitHub growth curves in open-source history.

marketTrajectory.headline: Claude Opus 4.7: 83.6% OSWorld
marketTrajectory.detail: Closed: Claude Computer Use, OpenAI Operator, Gemini Mariner. Open: Microsoft Fara1.5 (4B/9B/27B, 72% Online-Mind2Web), Holo3-35B-A3B (82.6% OSWorld-Verified).

hardwarePlatforms.name: Blackwell B200
hardwarePlatforms.advantage: Universal framework compatibility, mature CUDA ecosystem; GB200 NVL72 racks deployed at hyperscale (Stargate Abilene: 450K GB200 GPUs).
hardwarePlatforms.bottleneck: Severe scarcity through 2026; ~1,000 W per B200 (1,200 W combined GB200), driving liquid-cooling retrofits.

hardwarePlatforms.name: Instinct MI350X
hardwarePlatforms.advantage: Highest single-GPU memory volume — fits trillion-parameter models on one node; AMD locked 6 GW of OpenAI capacity (Oct 2025).
hardwarePlatforms.bottleneck: ROCm software stack still maturing vs CUDA; real-world MFU lags Nvidia under mixed workloads.

hardwarePlatforms.name: TPU v7 Ironwood
hardwarePlatforms.capacity: 4,614 TFLOPS FP8 per chip / 42.5 ExaFLOPS per pod
hardwarePlatforms.memory: 192 GB HBM3e per chip · 7.37 TB/s HBM bandwidth
hardwarePlatforms.bandwidth: 1.2 TB/s bidirectional ICI · OCS up to 9,216 chips/pod
hardwarePlatforms.advantage: Hyperscale linear scaling, 2× perf/watt vs Trillium; Anthropic committed $52B for up to 1M Ironwood chips.
hardwarePlatforms.bottleneck: Exclusive to Google Cloud; Pathways/JAX-XLA migration cost; full racks exceed 1 MW (liquid cooling required).

hardwarePlatforms.name: Maia 200
hardwarePlatforms.advantage: Vertically integrated with Azure; in talks to host Anthropic's Claude inference (~$30B Azure commitment).
hardwarePlatforms.bottleneck: Second-generation; production volumes still ramping vs Nvidia/AMD; H100/MI325X-tier performance, not frontier.

dataCenters.title: AI Data Centers: The Build-Out
dataCenters.intro: By May 2026, the silicon arms war became a data-center arms war. The bottleneck moved from accelerators to the gigawatts needed to power them — driving a $500B+ Stargate program, multi-billion-dollar Anthropic compute commitments, and a 36% jump in global facility-level power draw in two years.

projects.name: Stargate (OpenAI · Oracle · SoftBank · MGX)
projects.highlight: Abilene TX flagship: 1.2 GW, 450,000 GB200 GPUs, 6,400+ workers; first 2 buildings live since Sept 2025; remaining 6 by mid-2026. Total planned ~8 GW; >$400B already deployed.

projects.name: Anthropic Compute Stack
projects.highlight: Up to 1M TPU v7 Ironwood chips from Google; 5 GW of AWS Trainium capacity; Maia 200 inference deal in talks with Microsoft. Targets a $950B IPO in 2026.

projects.name: Custom Silicon Wave
projects.scope: Microsoft Maia 200 · Amazon Trainium 3 · OpenAI 'Titan' · Meta MTIA
projects.highlight: Hyperscalers de-risk Nvidia dependency. OpenAI's Titan (TSMC 3nm with Broadcom) targets H2 2026 mass production across 10 GW of Stargate. Maia 200 (Jan 2026): 216 GB HBM3e, 10+ PFLOPS FP4.

projects.name: Power & Sustainability Crunch
projects.scope: Nuclear pivot · liquid cooling default · 2× copper intensity
projects.highlight: Microsoft, Google, Meta sign nuclear PPAs (Three Mile Island restart, SMR commitments). Stargate Norway runs on hydropower; Abilene + Milam TX backed by SB Energy. 2024: 415 TWh global DC power → 945 TWh by 2030 (IEA, 2.3× growth).

economicsSplit.title: Open-Weight Sovereign Infrastructure vs. Centralized APIs
economicsSplit.description: Enterprise deployment patterns shifted decisively between 2025 and 2026, driven by a 13-fold reduction in operating costs and absolute data control.
economicsSplit.cagrDetails: Calculated starting from a 2025 baseline of $19.05 Billion with a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 21.1%, heading toward $50 Billion by 2030.

dimensions.sovereign: Low hosting costs ($0.26/M input, $0.39/M output) — 13x cheaper

dimensions.centralized: Transmitted over networks, subject to external policies
dimensions.sovereign: Fully on-premises, air-gapped deployment, secure local retention

dimensions.centralized: Subject to provider depreciation timelines & endpoint shutdowns
dimensions.sovereign: Frozen indefinitely, custom fine-tuning & local orchestration

dimensions.centralized: Simple integration via basic endpoints & prompts
dimensions.sovereign: High, requiring local clusters and machine learning engineering teams

dimensions.centralized: Network latency, concurrent user traffic spikes
dimensions.sovereign: Controlled locally, consistent performance, predictable throughput

industryShift.title: Software Industry Expectations Shift
industryShift.intro: In February 2026, autonomous agents (Claude Cowork, OpenAI Frontier Agent) demonstrated complex knowledge work without dedicated software interfaces. Wall Street erased ~$2T from per-seat SaaS in weeks — a sell-off named the 'SaaSpocalypse'. Software giants are now repricing from per-seat licensing to per-outcome / Agentic Work Unit consumption, while a pullback wave (Klarna, Microsoft Claude) is testing the limits of agent autonomy.

pricingShift.title: From Per-Seat to Per-Outcome
pricingShift.desc: The licensing model fractured: human-seat SKUs are flat or declining at large accounts, while consumption-based agent meters scale with workload volume — but raise concerns about cost predictability and runaway invoices.

rows.agentic: Agentic Work Unit (AWU), token/op meter, outcome KPI

rows.legacy: IT procurement signs annual seat count
rows.agentic: Line-of-business owner pre-approves usage budget per workflow
Variable COGS (model inference) eats gross margin unless self-hosted
Runaway agent loops; unbudgeted token spend (cf. Microsoft Claude pullback)

rows.legacy: Vendor sells more seats as customer hires
rows.agentic: Customer cuts seats; vendor cuts its own seats too (Salesforce support: 9,000 → 5,000)

giantMoves.headline: Bedrock AgentCore + AgentCore Payments
giantMoves.detail: AWS DevOps Agent · MCP Server (GA) · AgentCore framework · AgentCore Payments (May 7, 2026) lets agents pay autonomously via Coinbase + Stripe within budget. OpenAI models (GPT-5.5, Codex, Managed Agents) now on Bedrock alongside Claude. Andy Jassy reorganized AI leadership for unified focus across AWS, Alexa, retail, ads.

giantMoves.headline: AI ARR $37B / Maia 200 / pricing pivot
giantMoves.detail: Q3 FY26 revenue $82.9B (+18%); Azure +40%. Maia 200 inference chip (Jan 2026). GitHub Copilot pricing overhauled June 1; M365 pricing updates July 1. Pulled back from Anthropic Claude over cost curve concerns — flagged the 'AI coding budget problem' for the whole industry.

giantMoves.headline: Agentforce + −30% YTD stock
giantMoves.detail: 29,000 Agentforce deals; 20T tokens → 2.4B Agentic Work Units. Internal support headcount cut from 9,000 → 5,000; large-account seat counts compressing 10–15%. Benioff hired 15,000 'Agent Supervisors' — a job category LinkedIn did not have a title for in 2025. Stock is the worst Dow Jones performer of 2026.

giantMoves.headline: Compute capacity > product moat
giantMoves.detail: Anthropic: $52B Google TPU deal (1M Ironwood chips), $100B AWS Trainium commitment, Maia 200 in talks; targets a $950B IPO in 2026. OpenAI: $500B Stargate program; $300B Oracle cloud contract over 5 years; custom 'Titan' chip with Broadcom (TSMC 3nm, H2 2026).

pullbacks.name: Klarna Reversal
pullbacks.detail: Klarna eliminated ~700 customer service positions for an OpenAI chatbot (peak: 2/3 of all interactions handled by AI). CEO Sebastian Siemiatkowski boasted AI could 'do every human job'. By 2026, the company has reversed course on the most aggressive automation — quality and edge cases pushed humans back into the loop.

pullbacks.name: 42% Project Abandonment
pullbacks.detail: 42% of enterprises have abandoned at least one AI initiative — up sharply from prior years — citing runaway compute costs, unclear ROI, and reliability ceilings. Nvidia engineers are reported to say compute now costs more than their salaries.

pullbacks.name: Microsoft Claude Pullback
pullbacks.detail: Microsoft — owner of GitHub, seller of Copilot, $37B AI ARR — publicly tightened control over Claude usage in mid-2026, signaling that even the most committed AI vendor is wrestling with cost curves. CIOs are increasingly demanding token-level FinOps before approving expanded agent deployments.

engineersUpdate.title: What Changed for Software Developers
engineersUpdate.subtitle: May 2025 → May 2026
engineersUpdate.intro: Most 'AI for engineers' presentations either over-hype or skip what actually shifted. This is the structured update: skepticism first, then the five inflection points that genuinely changed daily work, with verified data, demo recommendations, and a concrete two-week experiment plan.

stats.label: Pro Devs Use AI in Workflow
METR-Measured Slowdown
stats.trend: Yet devs perceived themselves +20% faster — a 39pt gap

flowSteps.title: Set the Table
flowSteps.desc: Open with skepticism. Engineers are a hostile audience for AI talks — too many demos, too much hype. Lead with the METR study and the 42% enterprise abandonment rate to establish you're separating signal from noise.

flowSteps.title: The 5 Inflection Points
flowSteps.desc: Concrete shifts with verified data. Not 'GPT got better' — five structurally different things that didn't exist 12 months ago: shipping agents, the price collapse, reasoning as a knob, MCP standardisation, and open-weight parity.

flowSteps.title: Show, Don't Tell
flowSteps.desc: Live demos against your real codebase. Side-by-side a 2024 Copilot tab session vs. a 2026 Claude Code / Cursor Composer session. Connect Cursor to internal docs over MCP on stage. Run an open-weight model locally.

flowSteps.title: The New Disciplines
flowSteps.desc: What engineers should actually learn this year: eval engineering (TDD for AI), spec-driven development, prompt caching as cost engineering, agent orchestration patterns, and observability for agentic loops.

flowSteps.title: Reality Check
flowSteps.desc: Honest tradeoffs: where AI fails, the slop problem, junior skill atrophy, cost surprises from reasoning models, and DORA-2025's finding that AI adoption amplifies existing organisational strengths and weaknesses rather than fixing them.

flowSteps.title: The Two-Week Experiment
flowSteps.desc: Three concrete experiments with a $200/eng budget: replace your editor with Cursor or Claude Code for one feature, stand up an MCP server for one internal tool, and write evals for one AI-powered feature. Schedule the retro.

shifts.title: Coding Agents Went From 'Autocomplete' to 'Ship a PR'
shifts.summary: The single biggest shift. Twelve months ago: tab completion. Today: delegate a unit of work and review the diff.
evidence: Cursor Composer / Background Agents edit 8 files in parallel from a single prompt
Claude Code, Codex CLI, Aider — terminal-native; plan, edit, run tests, iterate without an IDE
Devin (Cognition) ships full PRs from tickets; company in $25B talks; ACU-based pricing
Qwen 3.7 Max ran autonomously for 35 hours with 1,000+ tool calls
Kimi K2.6 Agent Swarm orchestrates up to 300 sub-agents in parallel
shifts.implication: The operational model flipped from 'AI helps me type' to 'AI does the task; I review the diff'. This is closer to managing a junior engineer than using an IDE plugin — a different muscle most senior engineers haven't internalised yet.

shifts.title: The Price Collapse Rewrote the Economics
shifts.summary: Inference cost dropped 30–200× in 24 months. Patterns that were 'cute demos' last year are now in production.
evidence: Kimi K2.6 ties GPT-5.5 on SWE-Bench Pro at $0.95 / $4.00 — 80% cheaper
DeepSeek V4 Pro: permanent 75% cut to $1.74 / $3.48 (Reuters, May 2026)
DeepSeek V4 Flash: $0.14 / $0.28 — sub-cent-per-call territory
Prompt caching now standard: 40–70% additional cost reduction with no code changes
shifts.implication: AI in the inner loop of production code is now economically viable. Per-row, per-user, per-request LLM calls are no longer prohibitive — this is the cost dynamic powering the SaaSpocalypse.

shifts.title: Reasoning Models Changed the Design Space
shifts.summary: Test-time compute became an architectural primitive. You don't prompt-engineer harder — you write better specs and turn the thinking knob.
evidence: OpenAI o1 (Sep 2024) → o3 → DeepSeek-R1 (Jan 2025) → standard across all frontier labs by 2026
'reasoning_effort: high' is the new latency-vs-quality knob
Claude Mythos Preview leads SWE-Bench Verified at 93.9% via deliberation
Output tokens dominate cost — reasoning models emit 10–100× more tokens per response
Spec-driven development is now the dominant pattern (Thoughtworks, Martin Fowler, GitHub all converged 2025–2026)
shifts.implication: Prompt engineering matters less; spec engineering matters more. Cost engineering has shifted from input optimisation to output budgeting and prefix caching.

shifts.title: MCP Standardisation Is the Unsung Infrastructure Win
shifts.summary: MCP becoming an open standard in 2025 is the AI equivalent of HTTP becoming a standard in 1991. Integration went from bespoke to commodity.
evidence: 54,450 MCP servers indexed across registries (May 27, 2026)
Glama Registry alone: 50,000+ developers, 1M+ tool calls per month
AWS Bedrock AgentCore exposes MCP natively (May 2026 GA)
Microsoft, Salesforce, Anthropic, OpenAI all wrote first-class MCP support
A2A (Agent-to-Agent) follows for cross-agent coordination
shifts.implication: Your work shifts: stop building N bespoke AI integrations, start exposing your services over MCP. Your IDE (Cursor / Claude Code / Codex) speaks MCP — connect it to your wiki, ticketing, observability, and internal services with one config block.

shifts.title: Open Weights Closed the Gap
shifts.summary: Frontier-quality models you can run yourself. Compliance, cost predictability, and customisation all changed in the last 12 months.
evidence: DeepSeek V4 (1.6T MoE), Kimi K2.6 (1T / 32B active), GLM-5.1 (754B), Qwen 3.5 — all within touching distance of GPT-5.5 / Claude Opus on coding
Kimi K2.6 ties GPT-5.5 on SWE-Bench Pro under Modified MIT license
Run frontier-class in your own VPC: data stays on-prem, no token fees, fine-tune on your codebase
DeepSeek-R1 (Jan 2025): System 2 reasoning trained via pure RL at fraction of OpenAI's budget — proved you don't need a hyperscaler
90% reduction in compute costs reported when migrating from metered APIs to on-prem open-weight clusters
shifts.implication: Three things that didn't exist 12 months ago: regulatory-grade on-prem deployment, predictable amortised cost, and meaningful fine-tuning of frontier-quality models. For regulated industries this is the difference between 'we can't use AI' and 'we can use the best AI'.

demos.title: Side-by-Side Editor Sessions
demos.desc: 2024 Copilot tab-completion vs. 2026 Cursor Composer or Claude Code shipping the same feature. Don't pre-record — let the failures show too. The shift is obvious without arguing for it.

demos.title: MCP Connection in 2 Minutes
demos.desc: Live: connect Cursor to your team's internal documentation via an MCP server config. Show the model querying real internal context. This sells the standardisation story better than any slide.

demos.title: Reasoning Knob Comparison
demos.desc: Same hard prompt to GPT-5.5 with reasoning_effort: low vs. high. Time it. Compare quality. Show the cost in the response usage. Engineers will absorb 'output tokens dominate' faster than any explanation.

demos.title: Open-Weight on Your Workstation
demos.desc: Run DeepSeek V4 or Kimi K2.6 locally (or on a single A100). Same prompt, similar quality, $0 marginal cost. The 'we can't use AI for compliance reasons' objection dies on stage.

disciplines.title: Eval Engineering
disciplines.desc: TDD for AI features. Promptfoo, Inspect AI, Braintrust, Langfuse — all production-grade in 2026. Write golden datasets, attach LLM-as-Judge graders, run regression on every deploy.
disciplines.stack: Promptfoo · Inspect AI · Braintrust · Langfuse · Helicone

disciplines.title: Spec-Driven Workflows
disciplines.desc: Write the spec/test, let the agent fill the implementation. Thoughtworks, Martin Fowler, GitHub, Amazon, and a 67-source academic review all converged on this pattern in 2025–2026.
disciplines.stack: Spec → Plan → Implement → Verify · agent reads and updates the spec

disciplines.title: Cost Engineering
disciplines.desc: Output tokens dominate. Prefix caching pins prompts at ~10% of input rate. Anthropic, OpenAI, Google all support it. 40–70% cost reduction is achievable with no code changes.
disciplines.stack: Prompt caching · KV cache reuse · model routing · context compression

disciplines.title: Agent Orchestration
disciplines.desc: When to delegate, when to chain, when to use a swarm vs. single agent. Observability for agent loops is its own discipline — debugging an agent that calls 300 tools needs different traces.
disciplines.stack: OpenTelemetry traces · LangGraph · CrewAI · AutoGen · Claude Agent SDK

realityCheck.title: AI can make you slower
realityCheck.body: METR's randomized controlled trial (mid-2025): 16 experienced open-source developers, 246 tasks, on their own repos. Result: tasks completed 19% slower with AI tools, while devs perceived themselves 20% faster — a 39-point perception gap. Tool selection on demos and intuition isn't enough.

realityCheck.title: The slop problem
realityCheck.body: Vibe-coded production code accumulates technical debt invisibly. DORA 2025 found code review became the new bottleneck: 'we got faster at producing change, we did not get equally fast at understanding its consequences.' AI amplifies existing organisational strengths and weaknesses — it does not fix broken delivery systems.

realityCheck.title: Junior skill atrophy
realityCheck.body: When the agent always writes the code, when do junior engineers learn to read it? The skill that's appreciating fastest in 2026 is the one that's eroding fastest among new hires: careful, slow, line-by-line reading of unfamiliar code. Plan training paths for it explicitly.

realityCheck.title: Cost surprises
realityCheck.body: Reasoning models can blow your budget. A single complex Claude Mythos or GPT-5.5 'high effort' query can emit 50K+ output tokens — that's $1.50 per call. At scale, this dwarfs your input costs. Budget for output, not input.

actionPlan.title: The Two-Week Experiment
actionPlan.subtitle: Three concrete experiments with a $200/eng budget. Schedule a 30-minute retro at the end.

items.label: Week 1
items.title: Replace Your Editor
items.desc: Install Cursor or Claude Code. Use it as primary for one real feature. Track: time-to-PR, diff size, your review burden.

items.label: Week 1
items.title: Stand Up an MCP Server
items.desc: Pick one internal tool (wiki, ticketing, monitoring). Wrap it in an MCP server. Connect your IDE. Measure: how often do agents use it?

items.label: Week 2
items.title: Write Evals for One Feature
items.desc: Pick one AI-powered feature in your product. Build a 50-example golden dataset. Add LLM-as-Judge graders. Run on the next deploy.

presenterPlaybook.title: How to Deliver the AI Update
presenterPlaybook.subtitle: 20 minutes · Senior engineering audience · Skepticism-first · Demo-led
presenterPlaybook.intro: Engineers are a hostile audience for AI talks. They've sat through too many demos, are allergic to hype, and are deeply protective of their craft. This playbook is the operating manual for delivering the content in the main report in a tight 20-minute slot — fast pace, two live demos, no hype, no pre-recordings.
presenterPlaybook.audience: Senior engineers, mid-level engineers, and tech leads
presenterPlaybook.format: Live interactive demos · slides only for stats · share links not screenshots

preparation.title: Pre-flight live demos against your real codebase
preparation.desc: Don't pre-record. Run Cursor Composer or Claude Code on a feature in your actual repo the day before, so you know the rough edges. Engineers learn more from the failures than the successes.

preparation.title: Stand up an MCP server to one internal tool
preparation.desc: Pick your wiki, ticketing system, or observability platform. Wrap it in an MCP server. Have it ready to connect to Cursor / Claude Code on stage. This sells the standardisation story better than any slide.

preparation.title: Pre-load METR + DORA 2025 quotes
preparation.desc: Print or pin the exact quotes: '19% slower while perceiving themselves 20% faster' and 'AI amplifies organisational strengths and weaknesses, it does not fix broken delivery systems.' These earn credibility in the first five minutes.

preparation.title: Pre-approve the $200/eng experiment budget
preparation.desc: Get sign-off from finance / your VP before the talk. The two-week experiment lands flat without a real budget. Make the call-to-action concrete and immediately actionable.

qaPrep.a: Probably not in the way the social-media discourse suggests. The METR study shows even experienced engineers using AI today are sometimes slower, not faster. What is changing is the work mix: less typing boilerplate, more spec writing, eval design, and managing AI agents like junior engineers. Skills are migrating, not vanishing.

qaPrep.q: Cursor vs. Claude Code vs. Codex CLI — which one?
qaPrep.a: Cursor for an IDE-style experience with multi-file Composer edits. Claude Code if you want terminal-native, plan-then-execute workflows close to a senior engineer's habits. Codex CLI if you're fully OpenAI-native or want open-source. Try at least two for two weeks each before committing — they're different mental models, not interchangeable.

qaPrep.q: How do we know the AI's output is correct?
qaPrep.a: You don't, by inspection alone, at scale. That's why eval engineering is the most important skill on the list. Treat AI features like any other production code: golden datasets, regression tests, LLM-as-judge graders, automated evals on every deploy. Promptfoo, Inspect AI, and Braintrust are production-grade in 2026.

qaPrep.q: What about cost? Won't this blow our budget?
qaPrep.a: Inference cost dropped 30–200× in 24 months. DeepSeek V4 Flash is at $0.14/$0.28 per million tokens. The risk is not input cost — it's reasoning models emitting 50K+ output tokens per query. Budget for output. Use prompt caching (40–70% additional savings). Route to the cheapest model that meets the quality bar.

Both. Closed APIs (Claude Mythos, GPT-5.5) for the highest-stakes reasoning. Open weights (DeepSeek V4, Kimi K2.6, GLM-5.1) for cost-sensitive bulk traffic, regulated workloads, and fine-tuning on your codebase. The frontier gap closed in the last 12 months — there's no longer a quality ceiling on self-hosting.

qaPrep.q: What about juniors? Are we destroying our pipeline?
qaPrep.a: Genuine risk. Plan training paths explicitly: pair junior engineers with seniors on AI-generated code reviews, require them to read and explain agent-written PRs before merging, rotate them through eval-writing duty. The skill that's appreciating fastest is also the one eroding fastest in new hires — careful, slow, line-by-line reading of unfamiliar code.

interactiveDemos.title: Show, Don't Tell · Four Working Demos
interactiveDemos.subtitle: Don't pre-record. Run these live on stage. Each demo embodies one of the inflection points and works entirely in-browser — no API keys, no network, full responsiveness. Skim the recipe, then click Run.

agentDemo.headline: Watch a multi-agent loop solve a real coding task
agentDemo.blurb: Kiro CLI's A2A protocol lets one orchestrator spawn specialised sub-agents in parallel. Pick a task and watch the loop: the orchestrator plans, then a researcher reads code, an implementer writes the change, and a reviewer validates. Each pane streams live tool calls.

api.text: Planning: this needs a query layer change, a route handler change, and a return-shape change. Spawning researcher + implementer + reviewer in parallel.
127 lines · GET /users returns full table · no LIMIT/OFFSET · no cursor
no existing pagination util · 4 other routes also unbounded
+ added findUsersAfter(cursor, limit) with WHERE (id, created_at) > (...) ORDER BY id ASC LIMIT $2
✓ 8 existing tests pass · ✓ 3 new pagination tests pass
Done. 2 files changed · 47 lines added · all tests green. Estimated PR size: small.

test.text: Test failure first. Read the test, read the code under test, find the divergence.
issue: jwt.verify is wrapped in try/catch but the catch resolves with req.user = null instead of throwing
handlers do not check req.user for null — they just access req.user.id

+ catch (e) { res.status(401).json({ error: 'invalid token' }); return; }
- catch (e) { req.user = null; next(); }
Done. 1 file changed · root cause was an over-permissive catch block. Reviewer validated full suite.

refactor.text: Refactor task. Need to map all callback usages, then rewrite, then verify behavior is preserved by tests.
found 7 middleware files · 4 use callbacks · 3 already async
uses jwt.verify(token, secret, (err, decoded) => …) · candidate for util.promisify
Done. 1 file refactored. Behaviour-equivalent — tests pass, no perf regression.

reasoningDemo.headline: Same prompt — dial reasoning effort from minimal to ultra
reasoningDemo.blurb: Modern frontier models expose reasoning_effort as a first-class API parameter. Higher reasoning means longer hidden thinking chains, more tokens emitted, more issues caught — and higher latency and cost. Move the slider, watch the same prompt produce qualitatively different output.
reasoningDemo.prompt: Review this auth middleware for security issues:

levels.label: Minimal
levels.output: Looks fine. One small thing — you should hash passwords before comparing.
levels.summary: Surface read. Misses everything except the most obvious.

levels.label: Low
levels.output:
Issues found:
1. Password compared in plaintext — should bcrypt.compare
2. No rate limiting — vulnerable to credential stuffing
3. user could be null if email not found — will throw on .password

levels.label: Medium
levels.output:
Issues found:
1. Plaintext password comparison (no bcrypt/argon2)
2. No rate limiting on login endpoint
3. Null dereference if user not found
4. Error messages leak whether email exists ('wrong' vs 'user not found')
5. JWT has no expiry — sign({id}) without exp claim
levels.summary: Now thinking about user enumeration and JWT lifecycle.

levels.label: High
levels.output:
Issues found:
1. Plaintext password comparison (no bcrypt/argon2/scrypt)
2. No rate limiting — credential stuffing possible
3. Null dereference if user not found (will 500 instead of 401)
4. User enumeration via different error responses
5. JWT signed without exp/iat/nbf — tokens never expire
6. JWT signed without aud/iss — accepted by any service with same secret
7. Timing oracle: == on password reveals matching prefix length
8. No CSRF protection if cookies are used to store the token
levels.summary: Catches timing attacks, JWT claim discipline, CSRF.

levels.label: Ultra
levels.output:
Issues found:
1. Plaintext password comparison — use bcrypt.compare or argon2.verify
2. No rate limiting — credential stuffing / brute force exposure
3. Null dereference (user not found → 500 not 401)
4. User enumeration via response timing AND error message divergence
5. JWT lacks exp/iat/nbf claims — non-revocable, infinite TTL
6. JWT lacks aud/iss claims — token reuse across services
7. String == is non-constant-time — timing oracle on password
8. No CSRF protection (if cookie storage)
9. No max email length — potential DOS via large email field hashing
10. Sign() likely uses HS256 with shared secret — symmetric key compromise affects all services. Switch to RS256/EdDSA with rotating keys.
11. Blocking db query — synchronous findOne under load will queue; use prepared statements + await with timeout. Also: this code as written is not async despite db calls likely being async — this is itself a bug.
levels.summary: Goes beyond conventional review — finds issues only deep code-review experts catch.

mcpDemo.headline: Browse the MCP server registry · 54,450 servers · build your agent's toolbox
mcpDemo.blurb: MCP became the universal language for connecting agents to tools in 2025–26. The MCP Toplist registry tracks 54,450 servers as of May 2026. Search, filter, and click to attach tools — watch the agent's context size and tool count update live.

servers.name: filesystem
servers.desc: Read, write, search files within sandboxed roots.

servers.name: git
servers.desc: Repo operations: log, diff, blame, branch, status.

servers.name: github
servers.desc: PRs, issues, releases, code search across GitHub.

servers.name: playwright
servers.desc: Headless browser: navigate, click, snapshot, scrape.

servers.name: chrome-devtools
servers.desc: Live Chrome session: trace, lighthouse, network.

servers.name: aws-cli
servers.desc: EC2, S3, Lambda, CloudWatch, IAM, RDS, …

servers.name: fetch
servers.desc: HTTP GET/POST with timeout + body parsing.

servers.name: web-search
servers.desc: Search the web. Returns ranked results.

servers.name: shell
servers.desc: Execute shell commands in a sandboxed cwd.

raceDemo.headline: Same prompt · four models · live race
raceDemo.blurb: Open-weight parity, visualised. Two closed-source frontier models race two open-weight models on the same prompt. Watch tokens-per-second, total time, and cost. The gap closed in 2026 — quality is now within margin of error; cost is what differentiates.

prompts.label: Generate a JSON schema for a user profile

rbac.gpt55: Role-based access control (RBAC) maps users → roles → permissions. A user gains capabilities only via roles, never directly. Three pieces: (1) roles are named groups of permissions; (2) permissions are atomic verbs over resources, e.g. invoice:read, invoice:approve; (3) bindings attach roles to users (or groups). Authorization checks ask: does this user have a role with permission X on resource Y? RBAC's strength is auditability — change a role, all its members update. Its weakness is role explosion when fine-grained access is needed.
rbac.claude:
RBAC is an authorization model where permissions attach to roles, not users. Users get capabilities by being assigned roles. Three layers:

• Permissions — atomic actions on resources (read:invoice, delete:user)
• Roles — named bundles of permissions (Editor, Admin, Auditor)
• Bindings — which users (or groups) hold which roles

Authorization at request time: does the requester hold any role granting this permission on this resource? Benefits: easy to audit, easy to reason about. Limits: scales poorly when access depends on resource attributes — that's when teams reach for ABAC or ReBAC.
rbac.ds:
RBAC = Role-Based Access Control. Authorization scheme that decouples users from permissions through an intermediate concept (roles).

Elements:
  permission ::= verb on resource type (e.g. read:order)
  role       ::= set of permissions
  user       ::= zero or more role assignments
  policy     ::= grants role on resource scope

Decision: user can perform action A on resource R iff user has role with permission(A,type(R)) and binding scope covers R.

Widely used in Kubernetes, AWS IAM (with ABAC overlays), enterprise SaaS.
rbac.llama:
Role-Based Access Control: users get permissions through roles, not directly.

Three concepts:
  1. Permission: an atomic action (e.g. 'edit document')
  2. Role: a set of permissions (e.g. 'editor' = read + edit)
  3. User: assigned one or more roles

When a user tries something, the system checks if any of their roles grants that permission. RBAC is widely supported, easy to audit, scales well for stable hierarchies. For dynamic per-resource rules, attribute-based (ABAC) is more flexible but harder to audit.

deflationaryImpact.impact: 40% contraction in hiring in AI-forward companies
deflationaryImpact.driver: IDE AI assistants (Cursor, Copilot) generating 46% of code in active files.
Urgent demand for custom fine-tuning and local RAG orchestration.

deflationaryImpact.baseline: 29% of total health tech capital deployed
deflationaryImpact.impact: 55% of total capital deployed ($14 Billion in 2025)
deflationaryImpact.driver: Accelerated shift toward automated clinical workflows and clinical copilots.
Automation of clinical coding, ambient scribe tasks, and claims processing.

deflationaryImpact.baseline: 2 administrative hours per active clinical hour
deflationaryImpact.impact: 50% to 70% reduction in document writing time
deflationaryImpact.driver: AI ambient scribing tools in clinical exam rooms.

deflationaryImpact.impact: Single-pass genetic prediction and expression ranking
deflationaryImpact.driver: MIT deep generative models automating biochemical codon optimization.

legalLitigation.case: The New York Times v. OpenAI & Microsoft
legalLitigation.parties: NYT Co. vs. OpenAI, Inc. & Microsoft Corp.
legalLitigation.dispute: Unauthorized model training, proof of 'regurgitation' (exact copying), user data destruction claims, and the May 2025 Federal preservation order for 20M user logs.
legalLitigation.resolution: Discovery phase. Large-scale dispute over user logs. Settlement expected under premium content syndication standard.

legalLitigation.dispute: Training deep models on copyrighted audio files, bypassing DMCA copy controls in training datasets.
legalLitigation.resolution: Motion to dismiss pending. Active dispute over whether training constitutes accessing data under DMCA § 1201.

legalLitigation.dispute: Structured, clean data training access for Google search and reasoning models.
legalLitigation.resolution: Commercial syndication model implemented, securing clean licensed data feeds.

legalLitigation.parties: Perplexity AI & Media Partners (e.g., LA Times)
legalLitigation.dispute: Copyright disputes over real-time content citation, scraping, and real-time synthesis of articles.
legalLitigation.resolution: Revenue-sharing framework paying publishers a share for content citations.

legalLitigation.dispute: Authorized database and archive access for model training.
